title: Managerial Decision Making
description: Test your understanding of relevant costs, incremental analysis, sunk costs, opportunity costs, avoidable costs, special orders, make-or-buy decisions, and capacity constraints.

questions.question: What is the main purpose of managerial decision making?
options: To compare alternatives and choose the option that best supports the organization's objectives
To record historical transactions in the accounting system
To eliminate all uncertainty from business decisions
To calculate taxes for the current period
questions.explanation: Managerial decision making involves identifying alternatives, evaluating their consequences, and selecting the most suitable course of action.

questions.question: When is information considered relevant to a managerial decision?
options: When it concerns the future and differs between alternatives
When it has already been recorded in the accounts
When it is allocated to a department
When it appears in the annual financial statements
questions.explanation: Relevant information must relate to the future and change depending on which alternative is selected.

questions.question: What is incremental revenue?
options: The additional revenue generated by choosing one alternative
The company's total annual revenue
Revenue earned in previous accounting periods
Revenue before variable costs are deducted
questions.explanation: Incremental revenue is the extra revenue that results from selecting a particular alternative.

questions.question: Which of the following is a sunk cost?
options: The original purchase cost of equipment that has already been acquired
The future maintenance cost of keeping the equipment
The resale value of the equipment
The purchase price of a replacement machine
questions.explanation: A sunk cost has already been incurred and cannot be changed by the current decision.

questions.question: What is an opportunity cost?
options: The benefit sacrificed when one alternative is chosen instead of another
A cost that has already been incurred
A cost allocated from another department
A future cost that is identical under every alternative
questions.explanation: Opportunity cost represents the value of the next-best alternative that is given up.

questions.question: A company is deciding whether to close a warehouse. The warehouse manager's salary would disappear, but allocated head-office rent would continue. Which cost is relevant?
options: The warehouse manager's salary
The allocated head-office rent
Both costs are equally relevant
Neither cost is relevant
questions.explanation: The manager's salary is avoidable because it disappears if the warehouse closes. The allocated rent remains and is therefore irrelevant.

questions.question: A company has already spent $500,000 developing a product. Future development will cost another $120,000 and is expected to generate $180,000 in future revenue. What should management focus on?
options: The future revenue and future development cost
The full $620,000 total cost
Only the $500,000 already spent
The accounting book value of the project
questions.explanation: The $500,000 is a sunk cost. The decision should be based on whether future benefits exceed future costs.

questions.question: A special order generates $90,000 in additional revenue and requires $62,000 in additional costs. No regular sales are affected. What is the incremental profit?
questions.explanation: Incremental profit equals incremental revenue minus incremental cost: $90,000 − $62,000 = $28,000.

questions.question: A shipping company can carry either Cargo A or Cargo B because vessel capacity is limited. Cargo A earns contribution of $160,000, while Cargo B earns $125,000. What is the opportunity cost of choosing Cargo A?
questions.explanation: The opportunity cost is the contribution sacrificed from the next-best alternative, which is Cargo B's $125,000.

questions.question: A manufacturer receives a one-time order at a price below its normal selling price. The company has unused capacity, and the order will not affect normal customers. What is the most important initial financial test?
options: Whether the order's incremental revenue exceeds its incremental costs
Whether the order price exceeds the normal selling price
Whether the order covers all allocated fixed costs
Whether the customer pays the same price as existing customers
questions.explanation: With spare capacity, the order may be attractive if its additional revenue exceeds the additional costs it creates.

questions.question: A company can manufacture a component internally for $34 per unit, including $9 of allocated fixed overhead. Only $2 of the fixed overhead would disappear if production stopped. A supplier offers the component for $29 per unit. What is the relevant internal production cost?
options: $27 per unit
$34 per unit
$25 per unit
$29 per unit
questions.explanation: The relevant internal cost is the variable cost of $25 plus the $2 avoidable fixed cost, giving $27 per unit. The remaining allocated fixed overhead is unavoidable.

questions.question: Using the previous information, what is the financially preferable alternative if there are no other relevant factors?
options: Continue producing internally because the relevant cost is $27 compared with the supplier price of $29
Buy from the supplier because $29 is below the reported cost of $34
Either alternative because the costs are identical
Buy from the supplier because allocated fixed overhead is always avoidable
questions.explanation: The relevant comparison is $27 to make versus $29 to buy. Internal production is cheaper by $2 per unit.

questions.question: A company has spare capacity and receives a special order for 2,000 units at $48 per unit. Variable cost is $35 per unit, and the order requires a one-time setup cost of $12,000. What is the incremental profit?
questions.explanation: Incremental revenue is $96,000. Variable costs are $70,000, and the setup cost is $12,000. Incremental profit is $14,000.

questions.question: A company is operating at full capacity. Accepting a special order would require giving up regular sales that normally generate contribution margin of $20,000. How should the lost contribution be treated?
options: As an opportunity cost of accepting the special order
As an irrelevant sunk cost
As allocated fixed overhead
As additional revenue
questions.explanation: The lost contribution from displaced regular sales is a future sacrifice caused by accepting the order, so it is an opportunity cost.

questions.question: A service division reports revenue of $500,000, variable costs of $310,000, and allocated fixed costs of $240,000. Only $120,000 of the fixed costs are avoidable. What would happen to total company profit if the division were closed?
options: Profit would decrease by $70,000
Profit would increase by $50,000
Profit would decrease by $190,000
Profit would increase by $120,000
questions.explanation: The division provides contribution margin of $190,000. Closing it saves only $120,000 of avoidable fixed costs, so company profit falls by $70,000.

questions.question: Why can a segment showing an accounting loss still be worth keeping?
options: It may generate contribution margin that helps cover unavoidable common fixed costs
All accounting losses are temporary
Allocated fixed costs always disappear when a segment closes
Revenue is irrelevant in keep-or-drop decisions
questions.explanation: A segment may appear unprofitable after allocated common costs but still improve total company profit by contributing toward costs that will remain regardless.

questions.question: Product A earns contribution margin of $72 per unit and requires four machine hours. Product B earns $50 per unit and requires two machine hours. Machine time is limited. Which product should normally receive priority?
options: Product B because it earns $25 per machine hour compared with Product A's $18
Product A because it has the higher contribution margin per unit
Product A because it uses more machine hours
Both products because their total contribution is similar
questions.explanation: When machine hours are constrained, products should be ranked by contribution per machine hour. Product B generates the higher contribution from the scarce resource.

questions.question: A shipping company can use limited dry-dock capacity for either Vessel A or Vessel B. Vessel A is expected to generate $300,000 of additional contribution after docking, while Vessel B would generate $240,000. If Vessel A is selected, what is the opportunity cost?
questions.explanation: The opportunity cost is the benefit sacrificed from the next-best alternative, which is Vessel B's $240,000 contribution.

questions.question: An external supplier offers a lower price than the company's relevant internal production cost. Which factor could still justify continuing internal production?
options: The component is strategically critical and the supplier has unreliable delivery performance
The historical cost of the production equipment was high
The internal department has allocated head-office costs
The supplier's price is below the company's total unit cost
questions.explanation: Quality, supply reliability, strategic dependence, and operational resilience may outweigh a modest financial saving.

questions.question: A manager continues investing in a failing software project mainly because the company has already spent $2 million on it. Which decision-making error is this?
options: The sunk cost fallacy
Management by exception
Contribution margin analysis
Opportunity-cost pricing
questions.explanation: Past expenditures that cannot be recovered should not determine whether additional future spending is justified.

questions.question: A company is deciding whether to replace an aging production machine. Which of the following should NOT influence the decision?
options: The machine's original purchase price paid five years ago
The expected maintenance costs of the old machine
The purchase price of the new machine
The expected resale value of the old machine
questions.explanation: The original purchase price is a sunk cost and cannot be changed by today's decision.

questions.question: A shipping company is considering outsourcing vessel maintenance to a contractor with lower prices but an inconsistent safety record. Which statement best reflects good managerial decision making?
options: Both financial savings and qualitative factors such as safety should be considered
The lowest-cost supplier should always be selected
Only accounting costs matter
Safety records are irrelevant if the contract is legally valid
questions.explanation: Effective decisions balance financial performance with qualitative factors such as safety, reliability, and long-term risk.

questions.question: A company chooses Product A because it has the highest contribution margin per unit, even though Product B generates much higher contribution per machine hour. Machine hours are limited. What mistake did management make?
options: They ignored the capacity constraint
They included sunk costs
They overestimated fixed costs
They underestimated depreciation
questions.explanation: When capacity is constrained, products should be evaluated based on contribution per unit of the scarce resource.

questions.question: A business rejects every special order priced below its normal selling price, even when it has significant unused capacity. What is the main weakness of this policy?
options: It ignores incremental analysis and may reject profitable opportunities
It always maximizes long-term profit
It prevents contribution margin from being calculated
It guarantees efficient use of resources
questions.explanation: With spare capacity, a special order may still increase total profit if it generates positive incremental contribution.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why managerial decisions should not rely solely on accounting reports?
options: Accounting reports often omit strategic, operational, and qualitative factors that influence future success
Accounting reports never contain useful information
Financial information is only relevant for tax authorities
Managers should ignore financial information whenever possible
questions.explanation: Financial data is essential but should be combined with operational, strategic, and qualitative considerations.

questions.question: A shipping company is deciding between two investments. One provides higher expected profits but also much greater uncertainty due to volatile fuel prices. What additional analysis would strengthen the decision?
options: Sensitivity or scenario analysis
Increasing depreciation expense
Calculating historical book value
Ignoring uncertainty because estimates are never perfect
questions.explanation: Sensitivity and scenario analysis help managers understand how changes in assumptions affect expected outcomes.

questions.question: Which statement best describes the relationship between quantitative and qualitative information in managerial decision making?
options: Both should be considered because financial calculations alone rarely capture every important consequence
Qualitative information should only be considered when profits are negative
Quantitative information should always override qualitative considerations
Qualitative information cannot influence business performance
questions.explanation: Strong managerial decisions combine measurable financial impacts with less measurable strategic and operational factors.

questions.question: A company accepts every project with a positive incremental profit without considering whether management has enough people to complete them successfully. Which important factor is being overlooked?
options: Capacity constraints and available resources
Historical depreciation
Allocated overhead rates
The company's accounting policy
questions.explanation: Even profitable projects may not be feasible if key resources such as labor, equipment, or management time are limited.

questions.question: Why are opportunity costs often overlooked in accounting reports?
options: Because they represent benefits forgone rather than recorded cash transactions
Because they are always equal to sunk costs
Because accounting standards prohibit their use
Because they only exist in manufacturing companies
questions.explanation: Opportunity costs are economic costs that are typically not recorded in the accounting system but are highly relevant for decision making.

questions.question: What is the ultimate objective of managerial decision making?
options: To make the best possible decision by evaluating relevant financial information together with operational, strategic, and qualitative considerations
To maximize revenue regardless of cost
To minimize reported accounting expenses every year
To ensure every department receives the same budget
questions.explanation: Good managerial decision making identifies the alternative that creates the greatest overall value while considering both financial and non-financial consequences.
